metadata.summary: Go testing idioms: table-driven tests, subtests, interface mocking, httptest, benchmarks, fuzzing, and parallel execution.

content.title: Testing in Go
content.summary: Covers Go's standard `testing` package patterns: table-driven tests with `t.Run`, interface-based mocking (manual + mockery), `httptest.Server` / `httptest.Recorder`, benchmarks with `b.N` and Go 1.24 `b.Loop()`, fuzzing with `f.Fuzz`, build tags for integration tests, and toolchain helpers (gotestsum, benchstat).

why.p: Go's test idioms differ from other languages: no test classes, subtests via closures, mocking by interface substitution (not monkey-patching), and benchmarks as first-class citizens. Misapplying patterns from other ecosystems (e.g., asserting inside goroutines, using `assert` instead of `require` for fatal checks) causes subtle failures. This methodology enforces idiomatic Go test style.

list.item: Writing any Go unit or integration test
Setting up table-driven tests with subtests for a new function
Choosing between manual interface mocks and mockery-generated ones

section.name: table-driven-tests-and-subtests
section.p: Table-driven test pattern, t.Run subtests, t.Parallel(), loop-variable capture fix, t.Helper(), and test naming.

section.name: table-driven-pattern
section.rule: Use table-driven tests for any function with multiple input/output pairs — avoids copy-paste and makes cases explicit.
Define test cases as a slice of structs with named fields — never positional anonymous structs.

section.name: t-parallel
section.rule: Call t.Parallel() at the start of each subtest to run them concurrently.
When using t.Parallel() inside a range loop (Go < 1.22), capture the loop variable explicitly to avoid data races.
section.note: Go 1.22+ fixes loop variable semantics — the tc := tc capture is no longer needed but harmless.

section.name: assert-vs-require
section.rule: Use require (testify) when a failure should stop the test immediately — nil pointer dereferences, must-have preconditions.
Use assert (testify) for non-fatal checks where you want to see all failures.

section.name: t-helper
section.rule: Call t.Helper() in test helper functions so failure lines point to the caller, not the helper.

section.name: interface-mocking-and-http-testing
section.p: Interface-based mocking in Go, manual mocks vs mockery, httptest.Server and httptest.Recorder for HTTP handler tests.

section.name: interface-based-mocking
section.rule: Define minimal interfaces in production code; test code implements them.
Keep interfaces small — one or two methods. Large interfaces are hard to mock and suggest a design problem.
Define the interface in the package that uses it, not the package that implements it.

section.name: mockery-decision-rubric
section.rule: Use manual mocks when: the interface has 1-3 methods, the test file is simple, or no codegen tooling is set up.
Use mockery when: the interface has 4+ methods, you need call recording, or the interface changes frequently.

example.description: mockery generated mock (installed: go install github.com/vektra/mockery/v2)
example.code: # Generate mock for an interface mockery --name=UserStore --dir=./internal/service --output=./internal/mocks

section.name: httptest-recorder-handler-tests
section.rule: Use httptest.NewRecorder() to test HTTP handlers without starting a real server.

section.name: httptest-server-client-tests
section.rule: Use httptest.NewServer() to test HTTP clients against a real local server.

section.name: benchmarks-fuzzing-and-build-tags
section.p: Benchmark structure, b.N vs b.Loop() (Go 1.24), benchstat comparison workflow, fuzzing with f.Fuzz, and build tags for integration tests.

section.name: benchmark-structure
section.rule: Benchmark functions start with Benchmark, not Test. Run with go test -bench=.
Loop over b.N iterations — the testing framework adjusts N until stable timing is achieved.

section.name: go-1-24-b-loop
section.rule: Go 1.24 introduces b.Loop() as an alternative to for i := 0; i < b.N; i++. Prefer it in new code.

section.name: benchstat-workflow
section.rule: Use benchstat to compare benchmarks before/after a change — do not eyeball ns/op numbers.

example.code:
# Before change go test -bench=BenchmarkParseJSON -count=10 > old.txt
# After change go test -bench=BenchmarkParseJSON -count=10 > new.txt
# Compare benchstat old.txt new.txt

section.name: fuzzing
section.rule: Fuzz functions start with Fuzz and call f.Add() with seed corpus values.
Run fuzz tests with go test -fuzz=FuzzFunctionName to discover unexpected panics and crashes.
Fuzz-generated failing inputs are saved to testdata/fuzz/ and replayed on subsequent go test runs as regression tests.

section.name: build-tags-for-integration-tests
section.rule: Use build tags to separate integration tests from unit tests — prevents slow tests from running in the fast feedback loop.
